title: Electron configuration — why the periodic table is shaped the way it is
examTip: The number of outer-shell electrons = the group number (groups 1–7). The number of occupied shells = the period number. These two rules unlock the whole periodic table.
introTips: Have you ever wondered why elements in the same column of the periodic table behave so similarly? It's all about electrons.
The way electrons are arranged in shells around the nucleus completely determines how an element reacts.
Master electron configuration and you'll understand why sodium explodes in water, why noble gases are unreactive, and why ionic and covalent bonding happen the way they do.
intro: Why does sodium react violently with water while neon just floats through it completely unaffected? Why do chlorine and fluorine — two completely different elements — behave so similarly? The answer is electron configuration. The arrangement of electrons in shells around the nucleus is what controls every chemical reaction, every bond formed, and every property an element has. The periodic table isn't just a list of elements — it's a map of electron arrangements. Once you understand how electrons fill shells, the entire structure of the periodic table makes complete sense.

keyPoints.heading: Electron shells
keyPoints.content: Electrons occupy shells (also called energy levels) around the nucleus. Electrons always fill the lowest available energy shell first — they go as close to the nucleus as they can. Each shell has a maximum number of electrons it can hold: Shell 1 (innermost, lowest energy) = maximum 2 electrons; Shell 2 = maximum 8 electrons; Shell 3 = maximum 8 electrons (at GCSE level). Writing electron configuration: count the total number of electrons (= atomic number for a neutral atom), then fill the shells in order. Examples: hydrogen (1e): 1. Helium (2e): 2. Lithium (3e): 2,1. Carbon (6e): 2,4. Sodium (11e): 2,8,1. Chlorine (17e): 2,8,7. Argon (18e): 2,8,8. Calcium (20e): 2,8,8,2. The outer shell electrons are called valence electrons — they are the ones involved in bonding and reactions.
writeBullets: Shells = energy levels; electrons fill lowest energy shell first
Shell 1: max 2 electrons; Shell 2: max 8; Shell 3: max 8 (GCSE)
Write configuration by filling in order: e.g. Na (11e) = 2,8,1
Chlorine (17e) = 2,8,7; Calcium (20e) = 2,8,8,2
Outer shell electrons = valence electrons; these control bonding
A full outer shell = stable (like noble gases)

keyTerms.def: An energy level occupied by electrons around the nucleus; shells fill from the inside outwards
The arrangement of electrons in shells, written as numbers separated by commas (e.g. 2,8,1 for sodium)
The electrons in the outermost shell; these are involved in bonding and determine chemical properties

keyPoints.memTip: Fill shells like seats on a bus — front seats (inner shells) fill up first, then you go to the back. Shell 1 fits 2, shell 2 fits 8, shell 3 fits 8.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Make sure Mabel can write electron configurations for the first 20 elements fluently. This is foundational for every bonding topic that follows.

keyPoints.heading: Electron configuration and the periodic table
keyPoints.content: The periodic table's structure is a direct consequence of electron configuration. Elements in the same group (column) have the same number of outer-shell electrons → same chemical properties. Group 1 (alkali metals — Li, Na, K): all have 1 outer electron → all react vigorously with water, all form +1 ions. Group 7 (halogens — F, Cl, Br, I): all have 7 outer electrons → all form -1 ions, all react with metals to form salts. Group 0 (noble gases — He, Ne, Ar): all have full outer shells (He: 2; Ne: 2,8; Ar: 2,8,8) → all unreactive. The period number tells you how many electron shells are filled — Period 2 elements (Li to Ne) have 2 occupied shells; Period 3 elements (Na to Ar) have 3 occupied shells. This is why the periodic table has 2 elements in the first row (filling shell 1) and 8 in the second and third rows (filling shell 2 or 3).
writeBullets: Same group = same number of outer electrons = similar chemical properties
Group 1: 1 outer electron → react with water, form +1 ions
Group 7: 7 outer electrons → form -1 ions, react with metals
Group 0: full outer shells → unreactive (stable electron configuration)
Period number = number of occupied electron shells
Period 2 = 2 shells; Period 3 = 3 shells; explains the table's row structure

keyTerms.def: A vertical column in the periodic table; all elements in a group have the same number of outer-shell electrons
A horizontal row in the periodic table; the period number = number of occupied electron shells

keyPoints.memTip: GROUP = outer electrons (group 1 → 1 outer e). PERIOD = number of shells (period 3 → 3 shells). Two simple rules to decode the whole table.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is the core idea students often learn as a fact without understanding WHY. Help Mabel see that the periodic table literally maps electron configurations — each new period starts a new shell.

keyPoints.heading: How electron configuration drives bonding
keyPoints.content: Atoms react to achieve a full outer electron shell — the same electron arrangement as a noble gas. This single driving force explains virtually all of chemistry. Ionic bonding: a metal with few outer electrons gives them away to a non-metal that needs a few electrons to complete its outer shell. Both end up with full outer shells. Example: sodium (2,8,1) gives 1 electron to chlorine (2,8,7) → Na⁺ (2,8) and Cl⁻ (2,8,8). Both now have full outer shells. Covalent bonding: two non-metals each contribute electrons to a shared pair — both atoms effectively 'count' the shared electrons as their own, filling their outer shells without fully transferring them. Example: H₂O — oxygen (2,6) shares 2 electron pairs, one with each hydrogen → oxygen effectively has 8 outer electrons, each hydrogen has 2. Metallic bonding: metals have few outer electrons which are released into a 'sea of delocalised electrons' — the positive ions are held together by attraction to this electron sea. The more outer electrons released, the stronger the metallic bonding.
writeBullets: Atoms react to achieve a full outer shell (noble gas configuration)
Ionic: metal GIVES electrons to non-metal; both get full outer shells
Covalent: non-metals SHARE electron pairs; both 'count' shared electrons
H₂O: O shares 2 pairs with 2 H atoms; O effectively has 8, each H has 2
Metallic: outer electrons released into delocalised sea; ions held by electrostatic attraction

keyTerms.def: Transfer of electrons from a metal to a non-metal; both ions achieve full outer shells
Sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms; both atoms effectively fill their outer shells
Attraction between positive metal ions and a sea of delocalised electrons released from the outer shells of metal atoms

keyPoints.memTip: IONIC = GIVE (metal gives to non-metal). COVALENT = SHARE (both non-metals share). METALLIC = RELEASE (metal releases electrons into a sea). The goal is always the same: full outer shell.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise that the 'full outer shell' drive is the reason for all three types of bonding. Understanding this deeply means the bonding topics in C2 will feel logical rather than arbitrary.

keyPoints.heading: Dot-and-cross diagrams
keyPoints.content: Dot-and-cross diagrams are used to show the arrangement of electrons in atoms, ions and molecules. The conventions: draw shells as concentric circles around the nucleus symbol; represent electrons from one atom as dots (•) and from the other as crosses (×) — this shows which atom each electron originally came from. For atoms: Na (2,8,1) — draw three shells with 2, 8 and 1 electron. For ions: draw the resulting electron arrangement and include the charge in square brackets: Mg²⁺ (2,8) — lost 2 electrons; O²⁻ (2,8) — gained 2 electrons. For covalent molecules: show shared pairs of electrons between atoms in the overlapping region of the shells. AQA commonly asks for outer shells only (no need to draw inner shells for each atom in a molecule). Key molecules to know: H₂ (one shared pair), HCl (one shared pair), H₂O (two shared pairs, two lone pairs on O), NH₃ (three shared pairs, one lone pair on N), CH₄ (four shared pairs around C), O₂ (double bond — two shared pairs), N₂ (triple bond — three shared pairs).
writeBullets: Dot-and-cross: electrons shown as dots (one atom) and crosses (other atom)
Ions: draw in square brackets with charge shown outside
Na⁺: 2,8 (lost 1e); Cl⁻: 2,8,8 (gained 1e); O²⁻: 2,8 (gained 2e)
Covalent molecules: shared pairs shown between atoms in overlapping shells
H₂O: 2 shared pairs + 2 lone pairs on oxygen
CH₄: 4 shared pairs; NH₃: 3 shared pairs + 1 lone pair; O₂: double bond (2 shared pairs)

keyTerms.def: A diagram showing electron arrangement using dots and crosses to distinguish electrons from different atoms
A pair of electrons in the outer shell of an atom that is not involved in bonding
A pair of electrons shared between two atoms in a covalent bond; each atom contributes one electron

keyPoints.memTip: For dot-and-cross: always count the total electrons first, then place them. Shared pairs go BETWEEN atoms in the overlap. Lone pairs stay on the one atom.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA often only asks for outer shell electrons in covalent molecules — students waste time drawing inner shells. Check the question wording carefully.

tables.title: Electron configurations of the first 20 elements

0.question: An element has the electron configuration 2,8,6. Which group and period is it in?
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! The number of outer-shell electrons (6) = group number → Group 6. The number of occupied shells (3) = period number → Period 3. This element is sulfur (S, atomic number 16).
0.wrongFeedback: Remember the two rules: outer-shell electrons = group number; number of occupied shells = period number. Configuration 2,8,6 has 6 outer electrons (Group 6) and 3 occupied shells (Period 3). That's Group 6, Period 3 — sulfur.

1.question: Why do all elements in Group 1 have similar chemical properties?
options: They all have 1 electron in their outer shell
They all have the same mass number
They all have the same number of neutrons
They all have 1 proton in their nucleus
1.correctFeedback: Correct! All Group 1 elements (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) have 1 electron in their outer shell. This outer electron is what reacts — so they all react in similar ways, losing that 1 electron to form +1 ions.
1.wrongFeedback: Chemical properties are determined by electron configuration — specifically the number of outer-shell electrons. All Group 1 elements have exactly 1 outer electron, which is why they all react similarly (losing that electron to form +1 ions).

3.question: In a dot-and-cross diagram for water (H₂O), how many shared pairs of electrons and lone pairs does the oxygen atom have?
3.correctFeedback: Perfect! Oxygen has 6 outer electrons (config 2,6). In H₂O it shares 1 pair with each hydrogen (2 shared pairs) and keeps the other 4 electrons as 2 lone pairs. Total outer electrons on O = 2+2+2+2 = 8 (full outer shell — 4 pairs).
3.wrongFeedback: Oxygen has electron configuration 2,6 — so 6 outer electrons. In H₂O, it forms 2 bonds (one with each H), sharing 2 pairs. The remaining 4 electrons form 2 lone pairs. So: 2 shared pairs + 2 lone pairs on oxygen.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that shell 1 can only hold 2 electrons — students sometimes put 8 in shell 1. Shell 1 is full with just 2 (helium's configuration).
Confusing group number with period number — group = outer electrons (vertical column); period = number of shells (horizontal row).
Drawing electrons individually rather than in pairs for lone pairs in dot-and-cross diagrams — AQA expects lone pairs to be drawn as pairs of dots/crosses.
Thinking ionic bond formation requires exactly filling the outer shell — the key is that BOTH the metal ion and the non-metal ion end up with full outer shells after electron transfer.
Writing the electron configuration of an ion rather than the atom (or vice versa) — always check whether the question asks for the atom or the ion, and adjust the electron count accordingly.
examTips: To find the group of any element: count the electrons in the outer shell. To find the period: count the number of shells with electrons in them. These two rules work for all elements 1–20.

When explaining why Group 1 elements are reactive, say: 'Group 1 elements have only 1 electron in their outer shell, which they lose easily to form a stable +1 ion with a full outer shell — like a noble gas.'
In dot-and-cross questions, always count total outer electrons before you start drawing. This prevents you running out or having too many electrons.
Noble gases have full outer shells — that's why they're unreactive. Use this as a reference point: any ion with a noble gas configuration (like Na⁺ = 2,8, same as Ne) is particularly stable.
revisionCardBullets: Electrons fill shells in order from lowest energy (innermost) first
Shell 1 max = 2; Shell 2 max = 8; Shell 3 max = 8 (GCSE)
Number of occupied shells = PERIOD number
Group 1: 1 outer e → reactive metals, form +1 ions
Group 7: 7 outer e → form -1 ions, react with metals
Group 0: full outer shells → unreactive noble gases
Atoms react to achieve a full outer shell (noble gas configuration)
Ionic: metal GIVES electrons; Covalent: non-metals SHARE electrons
Dot-and-cross: dots vs crosses show which atom electrons came from
H₂O: 2 shared pairs + 2 lone pairs on O; CH₄: 4 shared pairs around C
